none: Passenger automobile average fuel economy, model years 1981-1984, Proposed rulemaking : environmental impact statement.

The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration proposed new fuel economy standards for passenger cars for model years 1981-1984. The goal was to improve fuel efficiency, reducing gasoline consumption and reliance on foreign oil. This would involve redesigning cars to be lighter and more efficient, using materials like aluminum and plastics instead of heavier steel. The proposal aimed to save billions of gallons of gasoline over a decade, benefiting the environment and conserving resources. However, it raised concerns about increased solid waste from plastics and potential noise from diesel engines.
Passenger automobile average fuel economy, model years 1981-1984, Proposed rulemaking : environmental impact statement.

The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration proposed new fuel economy standards for passenger cars for model years 1981-1984. The goal was to improve fuel efficiency, reducing gasoline consumption and reliance on foreign oil. This would involve redesigning cars to be lighter and more efficient, using materials like aluminum and plastics instead of heavier steel. The proposal aimed to save billions of gallons of gasoline over a decade, benefiting the environment and conserving resources. However, it raised concerns about increased solid waste from plastics and potential noise from diesel engines.

The proposed rulemaking for fuel economy standards during the early 1980s was part of a broader effort to address energy crises and reduce dependence on foreign oil. While the initiative aimed to enhance fuel efficiency and environmental benefits, it faced public pushback regarding concerns over increased solid waste and the implications of using alternative materials. Ultimately, the standards were implemented, leading to significant changes in automobile design and fuel economy regulations.
United States. National Highway Traffic Safety Administration

"The urgency of this need to conserve energy has also been clearly established. Domestic petroleum consumption has exceeded domestic petroleum production since the end of World War II." — Background Section

"A national program for conservation of energy is urgently needed because of the depletable nature and uncertain availability of most of the energy upon which the Nation relies for its economic and social well being." — Other Interests and Considerations of Federal Policy
